---
title: "Transfer & Archival"
subtitle: "Moving simulation outputs from Rockfish to a local workstation"
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
---

::: {.callout-tip}
## When to use this page

After a simulation pipeline run completes on Rockfish and the outputs
are verified in place, use this workflow to move the results to another analysis or long term storage env. The procedure is safe (full SHA-256
verification on both ends), efficient (a single `tar.zst` instead of
thousands of small Parquet files), and auditable (reproducible archive
hashes plus run-level provenance metadata).
:::

The pipeline writes a Parquet database at `{outputDir}/db/` plus one or
two top-level assessment TSVs. A production three-species run is several GB (~11-32 GB based on current tests)
and contains thousands of small Parquet files. This is a shape that punishes
naive `rsync` of the directory tree. This page documents the archive-first
workflow implemented by `pretransfer_manifest.sh` at the repo root.

## What is created by the staging step

For an output directory named `<RUN>/` (e.g.
`Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps/`), the staging step produces four
sidecar files in the parent directory:

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `<RUN>.tar.zst` | The compressed archive payload. |
| `<RUN>.tar.zst.sha256` | Single-line SHA-256 of the archive. Used as a fast atomic integrity check before extracting. |
| `<RUN>.sha256` | SHA-256 of every file inside `<RUN>/`, with paths relative to the parent directory. Used after extraction to verify every file byte-for-byte. |
| `<RUN>.manifest.txt` | Human-readable `key: value` metadata: created_at, hostname, user, pipeline git commit, file count, source bytes, archive bytes, tool versions. |

### The archive: `<RUN>.tar.zst`

The staging script compresses the output directory into a single zstd-compressed tarball. This process bundles the thousands of small Parquet files that make up the database into one downloadable and transferable payload.

The tar invocation creates a fingerprint so when the same script is run against the same, unchanged source tree, before and after the transfer the resulting archive is identical. 

The command used to create the archive in `pretransfer_manifest.sh` is:

```bash
tar --sort=name \
    --owner=0 --group=0 --numeric-owner \
    --mtime='UTC 2020-01-01' \
    -cf - <RUN>/
```

`--sort=name` imposes lexicographic entry order regardless of filesystem
directory-entry order. `--mtime`, `--owner`, and `--group` strip
filesystem metadata that varies across machines (modification timestamps,
uid/gid). Together these flags create an archive 
hash that can be used as a fingerprint to ensure fidelity.

::: {.callout-warning}
## macOS: GNU tar required

GNU tar ≥ 1.28 is required. BSD tar (the macOS default) does not support
`--sort` and produces non-deterministic entry order, so the script
refuses to proceed without GNU tar. Install it with:

```bash
brew install gnu-tar
```

The script will automatically find it as `gtar`.
:::


### Archive integrity: `<RUN>.tar.zst.sha256`

Immediately after the archive is written, `sha256sum` hashes the
`.tar.zst` file and writes the result to `.tar.zst.sha256` in the
standard `<hash>  <filename>` format that `sha256sum -c` can verify
directly. This file is **the first thing you check in Phase C** — before
extracting — because:

- It is fast (one sequential read of the compressed file, no decompression).
- It catches transit corruption: a truncated transfer, a bit-flip in the
  archive, or a mismatched file placed in the staging directory will all
  produce a hash mismatch here.
- If this check fails, you know the problem is in the archive itself and
  you can re-run `rsync` without touching any extracted files.

### Per-file manifest: `<RUN>.sha256`

Before the archive is built, the script walks every file in the output
directory and hashes each one individually:

```bash
find <RUN> -type f -print0 | LC_ALL=C sort -z | xargs -0 sha256sum
```

The result is a line-per-file manifest in the same `<hash>  <path>` format.
This is checked in Phase C **after** extraction, and it catches a
different class of problem than the archive hash:

- **Partial extraction** — if the decompressor was interrupted, some files
  will be missing or truncated; their per-file hashes will fail.
- **Bit rot after extraction** — storage problems on the local disk can
  corrupt individual files without touching the archive.
- **Accidental edits** — any file modified after extraction (accidentally
  overwritten, truncated, or edited) will surface immediately.

The archive hash and the per-file manifest are complementary. The archive
hash tells you the payload arrived intact; the per-file manifest tells you
every file inside it arrived intact after extraction. Both must pass
before it is safe to clean up scratch.

### Run metadata: `<RUN>.manifest.txt`

A plain-text `key: value` record written at staging time:

```
output_dir:       /vast/eande106/<user>/Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps
created_at:       2026-07-08T14:32:11Z
hostname:         login01.rockfish.jhu.edu
user:             mckeowr1
pipeline_commit:  63cdacba...
file_count:       14382
source_bytes:     11492301824
archive_path:     /vast/.../Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps.tar.zst
archive_bytes:    9103421440
archive_sha256:   a3f1c7...
manifest:         /vast/.../Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps.sha256
tar_version:      tar (GNU tar) 1.34
zstd_version:     1.5.5
```

This file is human-readable provenance: it links the archive to the exact
pipeline commit it came from, the machine and user that staged it, and
the raw file and byte counts. It does not participate in verification
(it is not itself hashed), but it is the record you keep long-term to
answer "where did this database come from?" or "was this archive produced
from a reproducible pipeline run?"

### Two integrity layers, by design

The archive hash and the per-file manifest guard against different failure
modes at different points in the workflow:

| Layer | When checked | What it catches |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|
| `<RUN>.tar.zst.sha256` | Before extraction (Phase C step 1) | Transit corruption, wrong archive, truncated transfer |
| `<RUN>.sha256` | After extraction (Phase C step 3) | Partial extraction, local bit rot, accidental file edits |

See `issues/pretransfer-manifest-archive/design.md` for the full
rationale and alternatives considered.

## Phase A — Stage on Rockfish

::: {.callout-warning}
## Do not run staging on the login node

The staging step runs `tar --sort=name | zstd -T0 -9` plus a per-file
SHA-256 walk over the entire output directory. For a production-sized
run (~11 GB) this **pins every allocated core for several minutes** and
does ~20 GB of read+write I/O against `/vast`. That profile is not
appropriate for a shared login node. Run it on a compute node via
`sbatch` (preferred, non-interactive) or `srun` (for an interactive
session).
:::

### Option 1 — Submit as a batch job (recommended)

The repo ships a small SLURM wrapper that allocates 8 cores / 8 GB /
1 hour and forwards all arguments to `pretransfer_manifest.sh`:

```bash
ssh <user>@<rockfish-host>
cd /vast/eande106/<user>/nemascan-sims-nf

sbatch scripts/stage_transfer.sbatch Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps
```

Output and error logs land in `stage_transfer_<jobid>.out` / `.err` in
the submission directory. When the job finishes, read the `.out` file —
the tail contains the archive path, SHA-256, and the copy-pasteable
Phase B/C commands.

Override SLURM defaults on the command line if needed (e.g. different
account or more cores):

```bash
sbatch --account=myaccount --cpus-per-task=16 --time=2:00:00 \
  scripts/stage_transfer.sbatch Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps
```

### Option 2 — Interactive compute node via `srun`

For a one-off or when you want to watch the progress live, grab a short
interactive allocation and run the script directly:

```bash
srun -A eande106 -p parallel -N1 -n1 -c 8 --mem=8G -t 0:30:00 --pty bash
# now on a compute node
cd /vast/eande106/<user>/nemascan-sims-nf
bash pretransfer_manifest.sh Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps
exit
```

Either option prints (on success) the archive path, file count, archive
SHA-256, and a copy-pasteable block of commands for Phase B and C.

### Optional arguments

Both the batch wrapper and the direct script accept the same arguments:

```bash
# Stage into a specific directory instead of the parent of <RUN>
sbatch scripts/stage_transfer.sbatch \
  /vast/eande106/<user>/Sims_... \
  /vast/eande106/<user>/transfers

# Force regeneration of staging artifacts (bypasses idempotency guard)
sbatch scripts/stage_transfer.sbatch \
  Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps --force
```

The script is idempotent: re-running it against unchanged source prints
`staging is current` and exits 0 without redoing the work, so re-submitting
the sbatch job is safe.

## Phase B — Transfer to local

Open a terminal on your **local workstation** (not Rockfish) and pull
the four sidecar files with a single `rsync` call. The `--partial` flag
preserves interrupted byte streams so dropped SSH sessions resume
cleanly.

```bash
rsync -avP --partial \
  <user>@<rockfish-host>:/vast/eande106/<user>/Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps.tar.zst \
  <user>@<rockfish-host>:/vast/eande106/<user>/Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps.tar.zst.sha256 \
  <user>@<rockfish-host>:/vast/eande106/<user>/Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps.sha256 \
  <user>@<rockfish-host>:/vast/eande106/<user>/Sims_ce-cb-ct_nqtl1-50_h2grid_50reps.manifest.txt \
  ./
```

A ~9 GB archive transfers in 5–20 minutes over a campus network; YMMV
on home connections.

## Phase C — Verify and extract

Still on the local machine, in the directory where you pulled the four
files, run three commands in order. All three must succeed before the
transfer is considered complete.

### 1. Archive integrity 
Fast, catches transit corruption

```bash
sha256sum -c ${OUTDIR}.tar.zst.sha256
```

### 2. Extract 

```bash
tar -I zstd -xvf ${OUTDIR}.tar.zst
```

MacOS (install `GNU tar` via Homebrew with `brew install gnu-tar` if needed):

```bash
gtar -I zstd -xvf ${OUTDIR}.tar.zst
```


### 3. Per-file audit 

Catches partial extraction, accidental edits

```bash
sha256sum -c ${OUTDIR}.sha256 > ${OUTDIR}.verify.log 2>&1
exit_code=$?
passed=$(grep -c ': OK$' ${OUTDIR}.verify.log)
failed=$(grep -c 'FAILED$' ${OUTDIR}.verify.log || true)
echo "Verified ${passed} files — ${failed} failed. Log: ${OUTDIR}.verify.log"
[ $exit_code -eq 0 ] && echo "All files OK" || grep 'FAILED' ${OUTDIR}.verify.log
```

The full per-file results (every `: OK` and any `: FAILED` lines) are
written to `${OUTDIR}.verify.log` as a durable audit record. The terminal
only shows the summary line and, if any checks failed, the specific
failing files. A clean run produces no `FAILED` output and exits 0.
Any `FAILED` line identifies the specific file that does not match what
was staged on Rockfish; re-run `rsync` and re-extract, or investigate
local disk health.

## What's in the archive

The archive contains the output directory exactly as it looks after the
pipeline finishes:

- `<RUN>/db/` — the full Parquet database (see
  [Database Structure](database-structure.qmd) for schema details)
- `<RUN>/db_simulation_assessment_results.tsv` — the main assessment
  results file (always present)
- `<RUN>/simulation_assessment_results.tsv` — legacy R-based assessment
  (only if `--legacy_assess` was used; see
  [QTL Detection & Assessment](qtl-detection-assessment.qmd))

Scratch work-directory artifacts (GRM binaries, intermediate PLINK files,
VCF subsets) are **not** included — they live under `/scratch4/` and are
intentionally cleaned up after transfer.

## Phase D — Clean up scratch (Rockfish)

After Phase C reports `OK` for every file, it is safe to remove the
Nextflow work directory on Rockfish. The work dir for a production run
is 100+ GB of intermediate files that are no longer needed.

```bash
ssh <user>@<rockfish-host>
rm -rf /scratch4/eande106/<user>/nf-work-*
```

The scratch directory default is defined in
[`conf/rockfish.config`](https://github.com/AndersenLab/nemascan-sims-nf/blob/main/conf/rockfish.config)
(`params.scratch_dir = /scratch4/eande106`).

## Troubleshooting

**Staging step: "GNU tar (>= 1.28) is required"**. The script hard-depends
on GNU tar flags (`--sort=name`, `--mtime`, `--owner`, `--group`,
`--numeric-owner`) that BSD tar does not support. On Rockfish, `tar` is
already GNU tar. On macOS for local testing, run
`brew install gnu-tar` — the script finds it as `gtar`.


**Phase B: SSH disconnects mid-transfer**. The `--partial` flag in
`rsync` preserves the in-progress archive file. Re-run the same `rsync`
command and it resumes from where it left off.

**Phase C step 1: archive SHA mismatch**. The archive was corrupted in
transit. Re-run Phase B with `--checksum` added to force rsync to
verify every block:

```bash
rsync -avP --partial --checksum ...
```

**Phase C step 3: per-file SHA mismatch**. Identify the bad file(s) from
the `FAILED` lines. If only a handful fail, re-run `rsync` on the single
archive and re-extract. If many fail, check local disk health
(`diskutil verifyVolume` on macOS, `smartctl -a` on Linux).


## Reference

- `pretransfer_manifest.sh` — the staging tool (repo root).
- `scripts/stage_transfer.sbatch` — SLURM wrapper for running the
  staging tool on a compute node.
- [Database Structure](database-structure.qmd) — what's inside the
  `db/` tree.
- [QTL Detection & Assessment](qtl-detection-assessment.qmd) — schema of
  the top-level assessment TSVs.
- [HPC End-to-End Testing](testing-hpc.qmd) — broader HPC workflow,
  including pipeline runs and scratch management.
